clean_story:
Frozen water. Silent voltage.

The phrase ice’s hidden electricity refers to the faint electric charge that builds when ice forms or melts—not from batteries or wires, but from the shift of protons inside its crystal lattice.

Ice isn’t just frozen stillness. As water freezes, molecules lock into a rigid grid, but some hydrogen atoms (protons) hop between oxygen atoms like restless frogs on lily pads. This movement creates imbalance: one side of the ice gains a slight positive charge; the other, negative. Too weak to shock you, but measurable—like static cling on a balloon, only colder.

In nature, this matters in thunderstorms. Hailstones collide in clouds, their icy surfaces rubbing to separate charges. The result? Lightning. Lab tests show ice can generate up to 10 volts per centimeter when stressed—enough to power a tiny LED if scaled. Glaciers, too, "crackle" with charge as they grind over rock, though the energy dissipates fast.

Three related subjects are snowflake physics, storm electricity origins, and crystal defect behaviors.
